entry.title: Re: OAuth2 Token Generation Using IronPython in SAP CPQ
entry.content:
SAP CPQ provides APIs for OAuth2 token generation.

For calling APIs from IronPython scripts, you can use:

  • AuthorizedRestClient — supports secure credential storage via the Credential Management feature
  • mtlsRestClient — supports secure client certificate handling via the Certificate Management feature

These clients can be used to invoke the token generation API and subsequently call other APIs using the generated access token.

entry.title: Understanding Status Fields in Manage Sales Documents
entry.content:
The Manage Sales Documents app provides a comprehensive set of status fields to help you monitor and act on sales documents at a glance. These fields are available at two levels: header level and item level. Understanding what each field represents helps you quickly identify bottlenecks, compliance issues, or incomplete data in your sales process.

Header-Level Status Fields

Header-level fields reflect the overall state of the sales document. Fields marked "(All Items)" aggregate the states of all items into a single indicator. Fields marked "(Header)" or with no suffix represent the state of the header itself, independent of individual items.

 

GroupFieldDescription
Processing StatusOverall Status (All Items)Rolled-up processing status across all items
Processing StatusDelivery Status (All Items)Rolled-up delivery progress
Processing StatusCredit StatusCustomer credit check result
Processing StatusRejection Status (All Items)Whether any items have been rejected
Processing StatusApproval StatusApproval workflow status
Processing StatusOverall Block StatusCombined block status across all dimensions
Processing StatusDown Payment Status (All Items)Down payment fulfillment status
Processing StatusOrder-Related Billing Status (All Items)Billing progress for order-related items
Processing StatusPurchase Order Confirmation Sts (Items)Customer PO confirmation status
Block StatusBilling Block Status (Header)Whether a billing block is active
Block StatusDelivery Block Status (Header)Whether a delivery block is active
Block StatusOverall Supply Block StatusSupply chain block at header level
Completion StatusIncompletion Status (Header)Whether required header fields are missing
Product Compliance StatusProduct Marketability Status (All Items)Chemical compliance check
Product Compliance StatusDangerous Goods Status (All Items)Dangerous goods check result
Product Compliance StatusSafety Data Sheet Status (All Items)Safety data sheet availability
Product Compliance StatusEmbargo Status (All Items)Trade compliance embargo check
Product Compliance StatusLegal Control Status (All Items)Legal control check result
Product Compliance StatusScreening Status (All Items)Sanctioned party list screening result

Item-Level Status Fields

Item-level fields reflect the state of each individual line in the sales document. They give you granular visibility when the header-level view is not enough — for example, when only specific items are affected by a block or compliance issue.

 

GroupFieldDescription
Processing StatusOverall Status (Item)Processing status of the individual item
Processing StatusDelivery Status (Item)Delivery progress for this item
Processing StatusDelivery Confirmation StatusWhether delivery has been confirmed for the item
Processing StatusRejection Status (Item)Whether this item has been rejected
Processing StatusDown Payment Status (Item)Down payment status for this item
Processing StatusOrder-Related Billing Status (Item)Billing progress for this item
Processing StatusPurchase Order Confirmation Sts (Item)Customer PO confirmation status
Block StatusDelivery Block Status (Item)Whether the item is blocked for delivery
Block StatusBilling Block Status (Item)Whether a billing block is active on the item
Block StatusSupply Block StatusWhether a supply block is active on the item
Completion StatusIncompletion Status (Item)Whether required item fields are missing
Product Compliance StatusProduct Marketability Status (Item)Chemical compliance for this item
Product Compliance StatusDangerous Goods Status (Item)Dangerous goods check for this item
Product Compliance StatusSafety Data Sheet Status (Item)Safety data sheet status for this item
Product Compliance StatusEmbargo Status (Item)Embargo check for this item
Product Compliance StatusLegal Control Status (Item)Legal control check for this item
Product Compliance StatusScreening Status (Item)Sanctioned party list screening for this item

Header vs. Item — When to Use Which

Both levels are available as filter fields and table columns in the app. Choosing the right level depends on the question you are trying to answer.

 Header-Level FieldsItem-Level Fields
What they reflectState of the header itself, or aggregated across all items for fields marked "(All Items)"State of an individual line, which may itself reflect the cumulative state of its schedule lines
Best forHigh-level overview and list filteringDrilling into specific line issues

Example 1: Header and Item pair — Delivery Block

The Delivery Block Status (Header) and Delivery Block Status (Item) are independent fields that serve different purposes. There are three scenarios to be aware of:

  • Header-level block — The block reason is set at the header level. Use Delivery Block Status (Header) to filter for these documents and check the header block reason field to understand why.

Header Delivery Block Status on header level

Header Delivery Block Status on item level

Header Delivery Block Status on schedule line level

  • Item-level block due to a schedule line reason — The item's delivery block status is a cumulative reflection of its schedule lines. If a block reason is set on a schedule line, the item will appear blocked. To find the reason, you need to navigate into the schedule line details — the reason is not visible at the item level.

Item Delivery Block Status on item level

Item Delivery Block Status on schedule line level

  • Item-level block with no reason — An item can show a blocked delivery status even when no block reason is set anywhere. This happens when the item category is not delivery-relevant (for example, text items or service items). In this case, the blocked status is by design and does not indicate a problem to resolve.

Item Delivery Block Status on schedule line level with empty block reason because in this case item category CBTA is not relevant to delivery.

Example 2: "All Items" and Item pair — Rejection Status

The header Rejection Status (All Items) rolls up the rejection state across all lines — it shows Partially Rejected when some items are rejected while others remain active, and Fully Rejected when all items have been rejected. To find out which specific items were rejected and act on them individually, use the item-level Rejection Status (Item) when navigating into the document.

Partially rejected documents on header level - status for all items

Partially rejected documents on item and schedule line level - status for all items and individual item

Everything rejected documents on header level - status for all items

Everything rejected documents on item and schedule line level - status for all items and individual item

How to Use These Fields in the App

All status fields are available in two places:

  • Filter Bar — Add any status field as a filter to narrow down your list of sales documents.
  • Table Columns — Use the column settings to display status fields alongside your documents for at-a-glance monitoring.

To add a field, click Adapt Filters in the filter bar or the Settings icon on the table, then search for the field by its label.

entry.title: Migration/Cut-over handling for open 107/109 Goods-in-Transit process when 107 and invoice are post.
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community,

we are currently using the Goods-in-Transit / Origin Acceptance process with movement types 107 and 109, as described in the SAP blog “Goods in transit using 107 & 109 mvt types”.

The business scenario is import procurement with long lead times, for example containers shipped by sea. Financial ownership of the goods is transferred before the goods physically arrive at the receiving plant. Therefore, in the legacy system we already post the first goods receipt step with movement type 107. This creates the valuated stock / valuated GR blocked stock in transit and also creates the accounting document. The supplier invoice is also already posted and completed in the legacy system.

During cut-over, however, some of these goods will still be physically in transit. After go-live, the physical receipt at the warehouse should happen in the new SAP S/4HANA system. In the normal process this would be the second step with movement type 109, releasing the stock from valuated GR blocked stock / stock in transit into available stock.

The challenge is that the first step, movement type 107, and the invoice have already been completed in the legacy system. Only the physical receipt step is still open and should be executed in the new system.

My question is:

What is the recommended migration and cut-over approach for this situation?

More specifically:

  1. Should the open 107/109 items be migrated into the new system as open valuated GR blocked stock / stock in transit, so that movement type 109 can be posted after go-live?
  2. How should the accounting side be handled, considering that the FI posting from movement type 107 and the supplier invoice already exist in the legacy system and should not be duplicated in the new system?
  3. Is there a SAP-recommended standard approach for such cut-over cases, or is a workaround/custom migration approach usually required?
  4. Would it be better to complete both 107 and 109 in the legacy system before cut-over and migrate the stock as normal unrestricted stock only after physical arrival, or is it feasible to technically continue the open 107/109 process across systems?

Any experience, recommended migration design, or lessons learned from similar S/4HANA cut-over projects would be very helpful.

Thank you in advance.

entry.title: VL60 Performance issue (Extended Inbound Delivery Processing)
entry.content:
Issue:
In transaction VL60, the posting of Goods Receipt (GR) for inbound deliveries is taking more than 2 minutes.


Reason:

VL60 loads all inbound deliveries exists in the system. This issue occurred mainly for a particular supplier's goods receipt, where a larger number of inbound deliveries exist, unnecessarily increasing the selection size (open as well as closed deliveries - GM status C or A&B).

This is not a bug, unfortunately, it is simply how VL60 works.

 

Solutions:

1. Reduce the number of inbound deliveries significantly by archiving older inbound deliveries and purchase orders.

If it doesn’t work, then

2. Modify the standard customising:

  • SPRO-Logistics Execution-Service Parts Management (SPM)-Extended Inbound Delivery Processing (SPM)-UI Profiles-Profile Modules-Define Selection Profiles

For selection profile - VL60_ALL

Here you can restrict the selection of inbound deliveries using selection profiles that select e.g. only such inbound deliveries with open (or partially open) goods receipt status (goods movement status 'A' and 'B')

  • SPRO-Logistics Execution-Service Parts Management (SPM)-Extended Inbound Delivery Processing (SPM)-UI Profiles-Profile Modules-Define Filter Profiles

You can restrict the selection of inbound deliveries that select, e.g. only inbound deliveries with a certain goods receiving point or storage location.

Also, restrict deliveries based on the inbound delivery date from the current date. You can define whether the system is to display inbound deliveries with errors.

For Filter profile - VL60_ALL

  • SPRO-Logistics Execution-Service Parts Management (SPM)-Extended Inbound Delivery Processing (SPM)-UI Profiles-Profile Modules-Define Configuration Profiles

Mandatory setting -
Configuration profile – VL60_STD

Activate "Filter When Selecting Database"

 

Check the DB table - BORGR_DIALOG_CFG, there will be an existing profile setup for user.

Now in VL60, go to Extras->Options and set the field "Filter When Selecting Database", enter and then leave transaction VL60 with the green arrow.
Table entry BORGR_DIALOG_CFG will updated for user id as per IMG setting plant, delivery days, etc..

If it hasn’t been updated, then delete table entry for your user id via SE16N (debug) or
create a report –In SE38 as per below code.

https://me.sap.com/correctioninstruction/0002107876/19773/corrinsdetail/0001603260/19773/0020751258/0001/102/108/S4CORE/Code

 

But with this solution, it restricts the actual selection from the DB, so all the filtered-out deliveries will not be found in VL60. And it may not be suitable for all plants.

To overcome this issue, there is solution to create the UI profiles and assign only specific users. so that particular plant's deliveries will displayed to specific user. 

SPRO-Logistics Execution-Service Parts Management (SPM)-Extended Inbound Delivery Processing (SPM)-UI Profiles-Profile Modules-Maintain Profile

Here you can create custom profiles and assign them to users, but THIS SETTING DOESN’T ACTUALLY WORK AS DESIGNED, AND IT FALLS OUTSIDE OF SAP SUPPORT. The reason this specific logic originates from an Automotive industry solution. Not developed by a SAP component.

 

Below is the perfect and more targeted solution that would work for all customers.

3. Create a custom transaction and IMG setting

A new transaction like ZVL60 by copying VL60 in SE93.

  • Start Object: Program and screen (dialog transaction)
  • Program: /SPE/INB_GR
  • Screen: 100

SPRO-Logistics Execution-Service Parts Management (SPM)-Extended Inbound Delivery Processing (SPM)-UI Profiles-Profile Modules-Define Function Profiles

Create a copy of the Standard profile – ZVL60_ALL
In this setting, you can decide where the push buttons with which you call up the individual functions are to be positioned. You can choose for each function whether you want it to be represented by a push button, and if so, whether you want a push button with an icon, with text, or with both. If you have decided against using a push button as a shortcut to a certain function, you can still call up this function via the menu.

 

SPRO-Logistics Execution-Service Parts Management (SPM)-Extended Inbound Delivery Processing (SPM)-UI Profiles-Profile Modules-Define Object Profiles

Create a copy of the Standard profile – ZVL60_ALL

 In this setting, you can define for each object which screen areas you want to be visible for certain users, whether you want the views to be expanded when you call them up and which tab pages you want to have on the screen.

You can define customer-specific tab pages for each object and display them on the screen. You can also use the object profile to define for which users you want these tab pages to be visible.

 

SPRO-Logistics Execution-Service Parts Management (SPM)-Extended Inbound Delivery Processing (SPM)-UI Profiles-Profile Modules-Define Selection Profiles

Create a copy of the Standard profile – ZVL60_ALL

As mentioned above, restrict the deliveries based on the status

 

SPRO-Logistics Execution-Service Parts Management (SPM)-Extended Inbound Delivery Processing (SPM)-UI Profiles-Profile Modules-Define Selection Profiles

Create a copy of the Standard profile – ZVL60_ALL

 You can maintain how the system is to represent the individual objects in the tree. Your settings determine the column headers; you also define for each object which entries the individual columns contain, and according to which column you want sorting to be carried out.

 

SPRO-Logistics Execution-Service Parts Management (SPM)-Extended Inbound Delivery Processing (SPM)-UI Profiles-Profile Modules-Define Filter Profiles

Create a copy of the Standard profile – ZVL60_ALL

As mentioned above, deliveries can be filtered out based on receiving points, plants, storage locations and delivery dates. You also define whether the system is to display inbound deliveries with errors

 

SPRO-Logistics Execution-Service Parts Management (SPM)-Extended Inbound Delivery Processing (SPM)-UI Profiles-Profile Modules-Define Configuration Profiles

Create a copy of the Standard profile – ZVL60_ALL

This profile contains options for the display of inbound deliveries, shipments, shipment views and general settings for goods receipt.


You can insert the URL that is displayed in the document area. It can be a custom Fiori app, a third-party logistics url that can help to the GR processes. (This is not expected to improve performance, it is for informational purposes)


VL60 Screen:

 

It is not required to create all the profile modules, mainly the selection profile,  Filter Profiles & configuration profiles are enough to filter out the deliveries.

Once required custom profiles modules are created, they should be assigned to the common profile ZVL60 (Profile name should be the same as the transaction code)

SPRO-Logistics Execution-Service Parts Management (SPM)-Extended Inbound Delivery Processing (SPM)-UI Profiles-Profile Modules-Assign Profile Modules to UI Profile

 

 
When the profile type is “A Transaction profile”, then it will be applicable for all users. (Recomended)

 

When the profile type is “user-specific”, then users should be assigned to below -
SPRO-Logistics Execution-Service Parts Management (SPM)-Extended Inbound Delivery Processing (SPM)-UI Profiles-Profile Modules-Assign Users.

 

Once new transaction ZVL60 and IMG setup is completed.
Run the transaction and go to Extras->Options and set the field "Filter When Selecting Database", enter and then leave transaction VL60 with the green arrow.
Table BORGR_DIALOG_CFG will be updated with user name, new organisation and all IMG setting. 

Now it can be observed that performance has improved with the new transaction code.

entry.title: Re: BADI or User Exit for Routing
entry.content: If you are on S/4HANA Public Cloud, direct user exits/BAdIs may not be available — you would need to use In-App Extensibility (Key User Extensions) with a business rule or custom logic. In On-Premise or Private Cloud, you can try with User Exit EXIT_SAPMC29C_001 - Enhancement Spot CP_ROUTING_CHECK or BAdI CP_CC_SAP_ROUTING

entry.title: Introducing the Joule in Universal Model for SAP PaPM: When Your Model Finally Talks Back.
entry.content:
Let's Be Honest: Nobody Opens PaPM for Fun. 

You open it because you need  something. A cost allocation run, A report status, A function buried three navigation layers deep in an environment you set up six months ago. The system has the answer - it always does. The problem was never capability. The problem is the twelve clicks, four screens, and two filter panels standing between your question and that answer. 

What if you just... asked? 

"Run the overhead allocation function in the SXP environment and show me the event log when it finishes." 

Done. No screens-hopping. No clicks. No treasure hunt. 

That's the Joule in Universal Model - a conversational interface to one of SAP's most powerful platforms. Not a chatbot bolted on as an afterthought. An entirely new way to work. 

So What Does "Just Ask" Actually Mean? 

This isn't a search bar with better autocomplete. The Joule in Universal Model understands the system - its entities, its hierarchies, its operational logic. It knows what an environment is, how functions relate to processes, and what it means to "run" something versus "create" something. 

That distinction matters. Because when you type "Which functions ran in production last week?", the agent isn't doing keyword matching. It's resolving your intent against the underlying data model, querying the right entities, applying the right filters, and returning a structured answer - the same work that would've taken you four screens and a filter panel. 

The people who use this platform daily - modelers, process managers, analysts, auditors - are domain experts. The agent lets them stay in that lane. No navigation detours. No memorising screen hierarchies. Just the question and the answer.   

Two Modes. One Rule: Say What You Mean. 

Most AI integrations stop at search - a smarter lookup, a better filter. Useful, but incremental. 

Joule goes further. Significantly further. 

Ask Mode - Explore Everything, Risk Nothing 

Ask mode is read-only by design - and that design choice is what makes it so powerful. You can query anything: environments, functions, processes, reports, runtime data. Filter, sort, aggregate, drill down, pivot. Explore freely across the entire system, in plain language, with complete confidence that nothing will be modified. 

  • "Show me all active environments." 

  • "List functions in the SXP2409 environment, sorted by last run date and tell me what this environment is about." 

When a query returns more than fifteen records, the agent flags it - so you always know whether you're seeing the full picture or a subset. 

And if you attempt a write operation in Ask mode? The agent respectfully declines and directs you to Act mode. That boundary exists by design - it's a governance feature, not a limitation. 

Act Mode - This Is the Part You'll Want to Show Your Team 

This is where Joule moves from impressive to transformative. 

Act mode doesn't simply let you make changes through conversation. It orchestrates multi-step workflows  - the kind that normally require careful, sequential navigation across multiple screens - in a single conversational thread. 

Consider what that means in practice: 

  • "Create a report with a margin analysis page, add a cost-centre breakdown element, and activate it." - That's not three separate requests. It's one sentence. The agent resolves the dependencies, sequences the steps, and executes them in the correct order. 

  • "Create a team called Regional Analysts and assign reader access."  - Described in a sentence. Completed in seconds. 

What sets this apart is not just the ability to execute - it's the understanding behind the execution. The agent knows what depends on what, what order things need to happen, and where to pause for approval. You express the outcome. It manages everything between the intent and the result. 

You don't navigate to the workflow anymore. You describe it - and it happens. 

"An AI That Can Change Things" - Yes, Compliance Took a Deep Breath Too 

If you work in finance, you already felt a twinge reading about Act mode. Fair. Here's how the agent earns trust: 

Approval flows: Act mode includes Auto Approval - and it defaults to off. Before any sensitive operation, the agent pauses, shows you exactly what it's about to do, gives you a Verify link, and waits for your explicit Approve or Decline. Nothing executes without consent. 

Source citations: In both modes, responses link back to the actual PaPM entities and screens that informed the answer. Hover for a preview. Click through to verify. The agent doesn't ask you to trust it - it earns it by showing its work.

Event log integration: When an Act operation generates an event log, the agent surfaces a one-click button to open it - pre-filtered, in a new tab. For auditors and process managers, this isn't a nice-to-have. It's the compliance answer they'd otherwise spend ten minutes hunting for. 

It Actually Knows Where You Are 

A few context features that sound small but change everything in practice: 

Screen awareness: If you're on a specific PaPM screen and ask "What is this?", the agent knows where you are and responds accordingly. No need to re-explain your context. But ask a general question, and it won't artificially restrict you to that screen either - it knows the difference. 

Environment scoping with @mentions: Type @ in the conversation, pick an environment from the dropdown, and every subsequent query is scoped to it. For organisations juggling dozens of environments across regions, business units, or fiscal periods - this alone saves real time and eliminates the "wait, which environment?" back-and-forth. 

Beyond Text: Files, Images, Charts 

File understanding: Drop a PDF or .docx file into the conversation. The agent extracts content, searches it, and cross-references it against live PaPM data - with citations pointing back to the source. Handy when you're reconciling allocation rules against a policy document at 8 PM. 

Visualisations:  Ask an analytical question, get a chart - bar, line, scatter, heatmap, you name it - rendered directly in the conversation. No exporting, no switching to a BI tool. "Show me cost allocation trends by region over the last four quarters" returns a visual answer in seconds. 

Not Just What's Inside - What's Out There Too

Joule extends beyond your system. When web search is enabled for your tenant, a globe icon appears in the conversation input. Toggle it on, and the agent can pull in live web results alongside its system-native answers.

Ask something like "What is the latest OECD regulations for BEPS" and the agent returns relevant results with titles and URLs, each opening in a new tab so you never lose your place.

It's a small toggle with an outsized impact: the agent becomes a single point of access for both your live operational data and the broader knowledge ecosystem - documentation, community threads, release notes without ever leaving the conversation.

Make It Yours: Teach the Agent How You Think

Here's a feature that changes everything about the experience.

Not every user thinks in system terms. A financial controller doesn't want to hear about entity types and navigation properties - they want business answers in business language. A new team member doesn't want a technical walkthrough - they want clarity.

Joule lets you define Rules - persistent instructions that shape how the agent behaves, responds, and prioritizes every interaction. Think of it as setting expectations once and having them respected every time.

A rule can be as simple as:

"I don't know anything about PaPM. I just want to solve my business problems. Keep the technicalities out of it. Always focus on business outcomes and impress me with what's possible-without expecting me to understand the system underneath."

From then on, every response the agent gives filters through your lens. It stops speaking in system terms and starts speaking in your terms.

Setting it up takes seconds: tap the three-dot menu, open Settings, and define your rules. No configuration files, no admin involvement. Just a clear statement of how you want to work-and an agent that remembers.

This is what personalization looks like when done right. Not themes and color palettes-but an AI that adapts its thinking to yours.

Who Actually Benefits? 

 

 

Before 

After 

Financial Analyst 

Navigate five screens, export data, and build a pivot table 

Ask a question, get a filtered answer with a chart 

Process Manager 

Manually configure, trigger, and babysit processes 

Describe the workflow, confirm the steps, done 

Auditor 

Trace actions through event logs and screen history 

Ask what happened, get cited answers, click through to logs 

New team member 

Steep learning curve, heavy documentation 

Start asking questions on day one 

 

The Bigger Shift 

This isn't a chatbot bolted onto PaPM. It's a different way of thinking about the interface entirely - from "navigate to the answer" to "describe what you need." 

Every environment you've configured, every function you've built, every process you've defined - that institutional knowledge is already in PaPM. Joule simply makes it accessible to anyone who can form a question. When finance teams grow, rotate, and face tighter close cycles, that accessibility isn't a luxury. It's operational leverage. 

So the next time you're staring down a quarter-end deadline with a dozen screens between you and the answer - try a sentence instead. 

Ask the question. Let the agent do the navigating.

entry.title: Enhancements to IsPrimary Field in Emergency Contacts, Email Addresses, and Phone Records
entry.content:
Introduction

The People Profile in SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central is widely used to maintain employee contact details such as phone numbers, email addresses, and emergency contacts. Maintaining this information accurately is important to ensure proper communication and reliable employee data.

  • In earlier scenarios, when updating contact details, it was possible to mark multiple records as primary during editing. This required users to manually review and correct the data before saving
  • Now the system handles this behavior and found that the primary record selection in a much better way. The system automatically ensures that only one record remains as primary, which simplifies the overall process.
Process Flow Overview
The following diagram illustrates how the system manages the IsPrimary field while changing existing employee data from employee profile.

Overview the process change

When existing record is set as primary, it cannot be edited directly. If another record is marked as primary, the system automatically updates the previous one and shows a message to inform the user that new record added as primary and old one changes to field value as "No" primary.

This makes the process simpler, as the system now ensures that only one primary record is maintained without any manual correction.

Implementing SAP Successfactors Latest People Profile settings

Configuration Requirement

To change first record to primary we need to enable the Latest People Profile UI which is mandatory for the changes.

Navigation: Admin Center → Configure Latest People Profile → General Settings

Enable:  Latest Editing and History UI for Employee Central HRIS Entities

 

Update existing records

Step 1: Existing Record Already Set as Primary

While updating the employee profile for contact details system will show the record is primary Below screenshot shows an existing phone record marked as Primary = Yes.

Step 2: User Adds a New Record

If you want to add new record as primary and second record as not primary, then click to Add button and create a new phone record.

Step 3: New Record Set as Primary

Now, the user marks the newly added record as Primary = Yes.

A new phone entry is created alongside the existing one. With message update that the new record added as Primary record and old record auto set as "No"

New record added

Previous Experience 

In earlier scenarios, where multiple records were marked as primary during updates, especially when making quick changes. This meant we had to manually review and correct the records before saving.

From a support perspective, this often-caused confusion for end users and required additional checks or follow-ups. Maintaining data consistency depended more on manual effort.

Now the system automatically manages the primary record, which reduces the need for manual corrections and makes the process easier for users.

Conclusion

After trying this out in different scenarios, I found this improvement quite helpful in day-to-day use.

Earlier, maintaining a single primary record needed a bit of manual effort and attention, especially during multiple updates. Now, the system handles it automatically, which makes things much simpler and reduces the chances of mistakes.

From a user point of view, this makes the experience smoother and easier to understand, even for someone who is not very familiar with the system behavior.

Overall, this may look like a small change, but it makes a noticeable difference in maintaining clean and consistent data in the People Profile.

entry.title: POWL - How to configure a custom TM query view
entry.content:
This article is a continuation of POWL - How to configure a custom TM query

SAP TM users can have personalized views for their POWL queries. Such query views can be created by individual users and can be shared with other users or groups. Views configured directly in a higher environment can also be deleted/erased.

Now, let us see how to configure such views in development and transport them to higher system environments in cases where pre-defined views are to be assigned for different job functions, or when user job roles change or when a new user is to be onboarded.

Such query views transported to higher environments can be refreshed and loaded overnight using report programs that run as background jobs ideally before the users start their day.

Step 1:

Standard view –

Customizing –

Right click and call application in customizing mode.

Select a TR

Update config mode from A to X

       

System shows a message as “Customizing mode”

Step 2:

Add/remove columns, sort, apply filters or make changes as per requirement to set up the view and click OK.

Please note: the selection will still consider the no. of records based on the underlying query and the view is a top layer of the respective query to only sort, filter or display desired selection.

The standard view will temporarily postfix with an asterisk (*).

Step 3: Click on the view and -> “Save as” to create a new one.

    

Create multiple views as desired per business requirements.

Step 4:

Close customizing mode and refresh the browser tab to test.

Conclusion -

POWL queries and views can be tailored to suit individual user needs, can be set up directly in a production environment or can be transported from a lower system, optionally, by adding a layer of security roles/authorizations. Tailoring such query views based on user needs improves system performance and end user satisfaction.

Happy reading!

entry.title: I Rebuilt My Home Server Three Times in Six Months. Here's What That Taught Me About Data Strategy.
entry.content:
Last Christmas, I Decided I Wanted a Home Server 🎄

Not because I had a grand plan, the need had crept up gradually. My wife Rachel had spent years building a real physical music library she cared about, and she wanted all of it digitized and accessible from one place, independent of a streaming service that might not carry it or a platform she didn't control. On my end I wanted somewhere to organize our media and actually back up our personal devices properly. Two pretty straightforward problems.

What followed wasn't a clean build. I started with the safe choice, a consumer-grade two-bay NAS with a clean interface and a reputation for just working. It hit its ceiling within weeks. So I pivoted to a more flexible open platform that could run containers, support a growing stack of self-hosted services, and actually feel like a lab instead of a fancy hard drive. Better, but still reactive. I was optimizing each step without ever mapping the full journey. By month five I finally did what I should have done at the start: sat down, defined what I actually wanted to build, and committed to a real foundation. Three hardware generations and a rebuilt container stack later, I had the homelab I should have built on day one. If I'd started with a strategy instead of a stopgap, I'd have gotten there in a fraction of the time.

I'm telling you this because I see the same pattern in enterprise data strategy more often than I'd like to admit.

🔄The Platform Refresh Problem

In enterprise technology, a platform refresh isn't just about new infrastructure. It's what happens when the system you chose at one level of organizational maturity can no longer perform at the next. Storage needs grow. Compute demands shift. Workloads that didn't exist on day one become mission-critical by year two. That's not failure, that's growth. But whether that transition is intentional or reactive determines how much rework, disruption, and lost value you absorb getting there.

Here's the thing: modern cloud-native data platforms are explicitly designed to make starting small feel like the right answer. Low friction, fast time-to-value, easy to justify to a stakeholder. And that instinct isn't wrong, it's a feature of those platforms, not a flaw in your thinking. The problem is that rapid business demand has a way of outpacing architecture before anyone realizes a plan was needed. The reporting layer that solved one team's urgent need quietly becomes the foundation for the enterprise's broader data strategy. Finance wants self-service. Operations wants real-time visibility. The business demands AI-ready pipelines. And suddenly the platform built for the first chapter of your data journey is being asked to perform in the third, without the architecture to support it.

The scramble that follows is disruptive, and it's almost always traceable back to an early decision made without sufficient long-term thinking. Not because anyone was careless, but because the platform made planning feel optional at the exact moment when momentum made starting feel urgent.

In BDC conversations with customers and prospects, I see this constantly. An organization starts with a tool that solves the immediate problem. The data strategy matures. The gap between where the architecture is and where the business needs to go becomes impossible to ignore. The question is never whether you'll need to evolve, it's whether that evolution will be intentional or forced.

🧭 Building Toward a Business Data Fabric

Don't scope your data platform to the problem you have today. Scope it to the data organization you're building toward.

Whether you're starting with a datamart, a departmental data warehouse, a data lake, a data lakehouse, or some combination of all of them, map the steps toward a business data fabric as your north star. That doesn't mean building it all at once. It means every decision you make today should be traceable to where you're going, not just where you are. A proper business data fabric integrates data across the enterprise, supports governed self-service, enables AI-ready workloads, and scales with the business rather than against it. That's the architecture worth planning toward from day one.

Ask the hard questions about integration, governance, scalability, and AI readiness before you're staring at a two-year-old architecture that can't do what the business now requires. A clear strategy upfront isn't a guarantee you'll never evolve, it means every evolution is intentional rather than reactive.

💡The Honest Takeaway

I rebuilt my home server three times because I kept optimizing each individual step without ever mapping the full journey first. Each decision was better than the last. Some were genuinely necessary. But the value lost to rework, rebuilding, and course-correcting under pressure never appeared on my original plan, it only showed up later, when the gap between where I was and where I needed to be had already gotten expensive to close.

That's the universal truth buried inside every technology implementation I've been part of. The most valuable path isn't the one with the lowest entry point, it's the one where you arrive at the right answer with the least waste. I started with nothing, made real mistakes, and eventually got there. But I got there faster once I stopped asking "what solves today's problem" and started asking "what gets me where I'm actually going."

One of the best parts of my job is helping customers do exactly that. There's a better path to your AI and data strategy, one that starts by defining the destination first. A business data fabric isn't just an architecture pattern. It's the north star that keeps every step of your journey pointed in the right direction. ⭐

🔗Explore SAP Business Data Fabric

▶️Watch a Demo

entry.title: Re: Need Clarification For EKORG
entry.content:
In SAP standard design, an RFQ (Request for Quotation) is always created at the purchasing organization + company code + plant level. That means you cannot directly create a single RFQ that spans multiple company codes, because each company code is legally independent and has its own accounting.

Possible approaches are

entry.title: Re: Why is Process Free Form Payments (F2564) called "Onl...
entry.content:
Hi There,

  1. Why is it called "Online Payment Flow"?

"Online" in this context means: creating a payment without referencing an existing business transaction.

Specifically, it refers to:

 

Aspect What "Online" Means
Payment referencePayment created without referencing an invoice or open item
Processing modeManual, immediate payment entry (not batch/automated)
UIAvailable via Fiori apps ("My Free Form Payments", "Process Free Form Payments")
Process flowSkips standard AP workflow: no supplier master → invoice entry → payment program sequence

The key distinction is that you manually key in bank details (IBAN, country, bank account, or bank alias) rather than pulling them from an existing vendor invoice.

  1. Typical Business Scenarios for Free Form Payments

Free Form Payments should be used instead of the regular AP process (MIRO/FB60 → F110) in these scenarios:

 

Scenario Recommended? Reason
One-time vendors (non-recurring suppliers not in master data, not the OTV account - OTV account is not supported)YesSupports one-time customers/suppliers without full master setup
Employee reimbursementsYesPayment without existing invoice document
Tax payments / Government feesYesManual entry to government entity bank account
Urgent/emergency paymentsYesTreasury can make urgent payments "on as-needed basis"
Bank-to-bank transfersYesCash management: bank transfer between two bank accounts
Loan payments / Trade-related paymentsYesTreasury-specific payment types
Refunds to customersYes

"Payments for Customers" is a dedicated Free Form type

 

When to Use Standard Payment Run for Open Items:

 

ScenarioUse Standard Process?
Regular vendor invoices with PO referenceYes - Use Create Supplier Invoices → Manage Automatic Payments
Recurring vendor invoicesYes - Use Create Incoming Invoice → Manage Automatic Payments
Bulk payments to multiple vendorsYes - Use Manage Automatic Payments
Payments with existing open itemsYes - Use Manage Automatic Payments

Key Differences:

 

Feature Free Form Payment(Payment run for payment request - F111) Standard Payment run for open items (F110)
Requires existing invoiceNoYes
Batch processingSingle paymentBulk payments
Vendor master requiredNo (supports one-time)Yes
Manual bank entryYesFrom vendor master
Approval requiredYes (release the request)Depends on BCM feature
Best forUrgent, ad-hoc, one-offRecurring, bulk, invoice-based

The classic transaction or program to differentiate two processes: One is F110, another is F111.

Best regards,
Hongliang

entry.title: Story Reports - Creating a change audit report as a story report - Compare effective-dated records
entry.content:
Hi everyone,

I have created a report to show changes in compensation data for a single employee at a time. I am trying to "filter out" records that don't show changes in the specific pay components I'm looking at.

Current configuration:

The bar charts successfully shows the data, but since my data model comes from Compensation Information, it creates a new data point for every record in the portlet, even if the specific pay components I'm using have not changed.


So the bars are for records in Dec 2023, Jan 2024, Dec 2024,  Jan 2025 and Dec 2025. You can see that the second and third values are the same, as well as the fourth and fifth. That's because something was changed in the compensation portlet (= new record), only not the pay components I'm using. 

For clarification: Every copy of the compensation item is filtered to a specific pay component. Some columns in my table in the report are calculations using these components.

So again: I don't want to show a bar if the record does not present changes in the specific pay components I'm looking at. 

Is there a way to compare each record to its previous record?

I have found this post: https://community.sap.com/t5/human-capital-management-blog-posts-by-members/successfactors-people-analytics-stories-how-to-create-change-report-current/ba-p/13955924 

However, using this method one can only compare the latest record to its previous, not iteratively each record to their respective previous one.

Thanks in advance!
Ana

entry.title: Building Your First SAP Module Pool: A Step-by-Step Guide to Screen Painter & Menu Painter
entry.content:
In SAP ABAP ,Module Pool was honestly one of those topics that looked intimidating from the outside. Everyone talks about Reports and BAPIs — but nobody really walks you through building your own custom screen from scratch. So when I finally got my hands on it and saw my first custom login screen come alive inside SAP, I knew I had to document every single step. This is that documentation — turned into a guide I wish I had on day one.

What Is a Module Pool?

If you’ve worked with SAP ABAP, you’ve probably used reports and selection screens. But Module Pool programming is a whole different beast — and a powerful one. It lets you build custom, dialog-driven applications inside SAP with full control over the screen layout, navigation, menus, and user interactions.

At its core, a Module Pool program uses two key processing events:

  • PBO (Process Before Output) — Runs before a screen is displayed. Used to initialize fields, set menu status, and populate data.
  • PAI (Process After Input) — Runs after the user interacts with the screen. Used to validate inputs, handle button clicks, and navigate between screens.

In this guide, I’ll walk you through building a complete Module Pool application from scratch — including a login screen, a customized menu bar, subscreen integration, a table view, and even an F4 value help (search help).

Setting Up the Module Pool Program

Step 1: Create the Program in SE80

 

Navigate to TCode SE80, select Program, and give your program a name starting with Z (custom namespace in SAP). Right-click the program in the Object Navigator → Create → Screen.

Step 2: Create a Screen

 

Enter a 4-digit screen number like 0100, 0200, or 0300. Every screen in a Module Pool must have a unique 4-digit number.

Step 3: Set Up PBO and PAI Modules

 

In the Flow Logic tab of your screen, you’ll see the skeleton:

PROCESS BEFORE OUTPUT.
  MODULE STATUS_0100.
PROCESS AFTER INPUT.
  MODULE USER_COMMAND_0100.

Uncomment these lines to activate them. Double-clicking on STATUS_0100 will let you create the PBO module and choose where to store it (use the Master Program).

Customizing the Menu Area with Menu Painter

Step 4: Set PF-STATUS and Title Bar

 

Inside your STATUS_0100 PBO module, set:

abap

SET PF-STATUS 'ZMENU'.
SET TITLEBAR 'ZTITLE'.

Both names must be in uppercase. These link your screen to a custom menu and title defined in the GUI Status editor.

Your moment here — e.g., Did this trip you up? How long did it take you to figure it out?

Step 5: Create the GUI Status (ZMENU)

 

Double-click on ZMENU to open the Menu Painter. Give it a short description and choose Normal Screen as the status type.

Step 6: Build Your Menu Bar

 

In the Menu Painter editor, you’ll see sections for:

  • Menu Bar — top-level dropdown menus
  • Application Toolbar — icon buttons below the menu bar
  • Function Keys — keyboard shortcuts

Click the dropdown next to Menu Bar, name a menu (e.g., DEMO), then double-click it to add entries. For each entry, provide a function code (e.g., SE41) and a short text label. These function codes are what PAI catches with CASE sy-ucomm.

Step 7: Add Application Toolbar Buttons

 

Click a slot in the Application Toolbar, enter a Function Code (e.g., CTRL), then double-click to set:

  • Function text: EXECUTE
  • Icon name: ICON_EXECUTE_OBJECT
  • Information text: EXECUTION

Step 8 & 9: Assign Function Keys and Save

 

 

Map your function codes to F-keys (F5, F6, etc.) from the Assign Function to Function Key dialog. Then click Save → Check → Activate.

Step 10 & 11: Configure the Standard Toolbar and Title

 

 

In the Function Keys tab, fill in the Standard Toolbar entries (BACK, CANCEL, etc.). Then double-click your ZTITLE GUI Title and enter the text that will appear in the title bar when the report runs — e.g., SCREEN PAINTER.

Designing Screens with Screen Painter

Step 12: Set Up Your Database Table

 

 

Go to TCode SE11 and either select an existing table or create a custom transparent table. In the example, two tables are used:

  • ZMP_LOGIN — stores user credentials (PERNR, PASSWORD, WERKS)
  • ZTMP_FY25 — stores customer data (Name, ID, Phone, Address, City, etc.)

Step 13: Write the ABAP Logic

Your main program handles login validation and screen navigation:

abap

*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*& Report ZTHIRU_MODULE_POOL1
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*&
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
REPORT ZTHIRU_MODULE_POOL1.
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*&      Module  USER_COMMAND_0100  INPUT
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*       text
*----------------------------------------------------------------------*

DATA: WA_LOGIN TYPE ZMP_LOGIN.

PARAMETERS: USER TYPE PERSNO,
            PASSWORD TYPE CHAR10.

DATA: CUST_NAME TYPE CHAR30,
      CUST_ID TYPE CHAR20,
      CUST_NUMBER TYPE CHAR13.
DATA: WA_RESULT TYPE ZTMP_FY25.

DATA: DOC_CAT TYPE EKKO-BSART.
DATA: IT_EKKO TYPE TABLE OF EKKO,
      wa_ekko type ekko.

CONTROLS TAB TYPE TABLEVIEW USING SCREEN 0300.

SELECT SINGLE * FROM ZMP_LOGIN INTO WA_LOGIN WHERE PERNR = USER AND PASSWORD = PASSWORD.

IF SY-SUBRC = 0.
  CALL SCREEN '100'.
ELSE.
  MESSAGE 'Incorrect userid and password' type 'E'.
ENDIF.


MODULE status_0100 OUTPUT.
 SET PF-STATUS 'ZMENU'.
 SET TITLEBAR 'ZTITLE'.

 case sy-ucomm.
   when 'SE41'.
     CALL TRANSACTION 'SE41'.
   when 'SE51'.
     CALL TRANSACTION 'SE51'.
   WHEN 'ZTHIRU_TC1'.
 CALL TRANSACTION 'ZTHIRU_TC1'.
*     LEAVE TO SCREEN 0.
   WHEN 'CANCEL'.
     LEAVE TO SCREEN 0.
   WHEN 'DISP'.
     SELECT SINGLE * FROM ZTMP_FY25 INTO WA_RESULT WHERE NAME = CUST_NAME AND NAME = CUST_NAME AND PHONE_NO = CUST_NUMBER.
      IF SY-SUBRC <> 0.
        MESSAGE 'Entered ID is not available' type 'E'.
      ENDIF.
   WHEN 'GET'.
     SELECT * FROM EKKO INTO TABLE IT_EKKO WHERE BSART = DOC_CAT.
     CALL SCREEN '300'.

 ENDCASE.
ENDMODULE.

*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*&      Module  USER_COMMAND_0100  INPUT
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*       text
*----------------------------------------------------------------------*
MODULE user_command_0100 INPUT.

ENDMODULE.

*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*&      Module  F4_CUST_ID  INPUT
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*       text
*----------------------------------------------------------------------*
MODULE f4_cust_id INPUT.

TYPES: BEGIN OF TY_INP,
       ID TYPE ZMP_LOGIN-pernr,
       END OF TY_INP.

DATA: LT_VAL TYPE TABLE OF TY_INP,
      LT_REL TYPE TABLE OF DDSHRETVAL,
      LS_RET TYPE DDSHRETVAL.

SELECT PERNR FROM ZMP_LOGIN INTO TABLE LT_VAL .
  CALL FUNCTION 'F4IF_INT_TABLE_VALUE_REQUEST'
    EXPORTING
      retfield               = 'CUST_ID'
      dynpprog               = sy-repid
      dynpnr                 = sy-dynnr
      VALUE_ORG              = 'S'  " 'C'
    tables
      value_tab              = LT_VAL.
  IF sy-subrc <> 0.
* Implement suitable error handling here
  ENDIF.
ENDMODULE.

*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*& Module STATUS_0300 OUTPUT
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*&
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
MODULE status_0300 OUTPUT.
 SET PF-STATUS 'ZMENU'.
 SET TITLEBAR 'ZTITLE'.

 case sy-ucomm.
   when 'SE41'.
     CALL TRANSACTION 'SE41'.
   when 'SE51'.
     CALL TRANSACTION 'SE51'.
 WHEN 'ZTHIRU_TC1'.
 CALL TRANSACTION 'ZTHIRU_TC1'.
*     LEAVE TO SCREEN 0.
   WHEN 'CANCEL'.
     LEAVE TO SCREEN 0.
   WHEN 'BACK'.
     LEAVE TO SCREEN 1.
*   WHEN 'CANCLE'.
*     LEAVE TO SCREEN 0.

 ENDCASE.
ENDMODULE.

The STATUS_0100 PBO module handles menu-driven navigation using CASE sy-ucomm, calling transactions or other screens based on what the user clicks.

Step 14 & 15: Design Screen 0100 Layout

 

 

Click Screen 0100 → Layout to open the Screen Painter canvas. Use the Text Field element to add labels like NAME, ID, NUMBER, ADDRESS, etc. These are display-only elements — they carry labels but accept no user input.

Step 16: Add Input/Output Fields

 

 

Use the Input/Output Field element for fields where data will be shown or entered. Double-click each field and set the name to match your work area — for example, WA_RESULT-COUNTRY, WA_RESULT-ADDRESS, etc. This binds the screen field directly to your ABAP variable.

Step 17: Add a Push Button

 

Create a Push Button, name it DISPLAY, and assign a unique FCTCode (e.g., DISP). This code is caught in PAI to trigger the data fetch logic.

Step 18: Activate Everything

 

Activate all screens, GUI Status, GUI Title, and PBO modules before testing. In SE80, right-click and activate each object.

Step 19: Execute the STATUS_0100 (Direct Processing).

 

Step 20: Test

 

Enter the User & password as in the DB table

Step 21: Test

 

Enter the Values and Click the Display, the fields matching to that input will be fetched from the DB table and displayed here.

Step 22: Test

 

Now you can see the Menu area where your customized tools will be displayed.

Adding a Subscreen

Step 23 & 24: Create Screen 0200 as a Subscreen

 

 

 

Right-click the program → Create → Screen → enter 0200. In the Attributes tab, set Dynpro Type to Subscreen and save. No PBO/PAI modules need to be uncommented for a subscreen.

Step 25 & 26: Design the Subscreen Layout

 

 

Click Layout on Screen 0200. Add an input field (e.g., DOC_CAT for Document Category from the EKKO table) and a GET push button with function code GET.

Step 27: Call the Subscreen from Screen 0100

 

In Screen 0100’s Flow Logic, add the subscreen call:

PROCESS BEFORE OUTPUT.
  MODULE STATUS_0100.
  CALL SUBSCREEN SUB INCLUDING SY-REPID '0200'.
PROCESS AFTER INPUT.
  CALL SUBSCREEN SUB.
  MODULE USER_COMMAND_0100.

Step 28: Add a Subscreen Area to Screen 0100’s Layout

 

 

Go back to Screen 0100’s canvas, drag a Subscreen Area element onto the screen, and name it SUB. This is the container that will render Screen 0200 inside Screen 0100.

Your thought here — Why did you choose a subscreen? What was satisfying or tricky about it?

Step 29: Test Subscreen

 

 

Execute the PBO Module SCREEN_0100 and give the input fields and check whether the sub screen is first working or not.

Adding a Table View Screen

Step 30–35: Create Screen 0300 (Normal Type)

 

 

 

 

 

 

Create a new screen numbered 0300 with Dynpro Type Normal. Uncomment both STATUS_0300 and USER_COMMAND_0300 in the Flow Logic. Double-click USER_COMMAND_0300 to create the PAI module and store it in the Master Program.

Step 36: Reuse the Same Menu and Title

 

In STATUS_0300, use the same menu and title:

abap

MODULE STATUS_0300 OUTPUT.
  SET PF-STATUS 'ZMENU'.
  SET TITLEBAR 'ZTITLE'.

Handle BACK navigation with LEAVE TO SCREEN 1 (returns to the previous screen).

Step 37: Choose fields

 

Choose the fields from the table which you have already used for the subscreen_0200.

Step 38: Create IT, WA.

 

Creating the Internal table, Work area for the table .

Step 39: Write the Loop Logic

In Screen 0300’s Flow Logic, add:

PROCESS BEFORE OUTPUT.
  MODULE STATUS_0300.
  LOOP AT IT_EKKO INTO WA_EKKO WITH CONTROL TAB CURSOR TAB-CURRENT_LINE.
  ENDLOOP.
PROCESS AFTER INPUT.
  MODULE USER_COMMAND_0300.
  LOOP.
  ENDLOOP.

Step 40–45: Add a Table Control

 

 

 

 

 

 

In the Screen Painter canvas for Screen 0300, select the Table Control element from the left toolbar. Draw it on the screen and name it TAB.

Use Dictionary/Program Fields (F6) from the menu bar to pull in fields from the EKKO table — select columns like Purchasing Doc, Company Code, Doc Category, Status, and Created By. Place them inside the table control and adjust column widths.

Step 46 & 47: Configure Column Fields

Double-click each column field. Under Attributes → Program, uncheck Input Field and check Output Only — since this is a display-only table. Rename each field’s Name to match your work area (e.g., WA_EKKO-EBELN, WA_EKKO-BUKRS, etc.).

 

 

 

 

 

 

Step 48: Execute

 

 

 

Process on Value Request (F4 Search Help)

Step 49: Create the F4 Module

 

Create a PAI module F4_CUST_ID that uses the standard function module F4IF_INT_TABLE_VALUE_REQUEST to provide a dropdown of valid IDs from ZMP_LOGIN:

abap

MODULE f4_cust_id INPUT.
  TYPES: BEGIN OF TY_INP,
           ID TYPE ZMP_LOGIN-pernr,
         END OF TY_INP.
  DATA: LT_VAL TYPE TABLE OF TY_INP.
  SELECT PERNR FROM ZMP_LOGIN INTO TABLE LT_VAL.
  CALL FUNCTION 'F4IF_INT_TABLE_VALUE_REQUEST'
    EXPORTING
      retfield  = 'CUST_ID'
      dynpprog  = sy-repid
      dynpnr    = sy-dynnr
      VALUE_ORG = 'S'
    TABLES
      value_tab = LT_VAL.
ENDMODULE.

Step 50: Add the POVR Block to Screen 0100’s Flow Logic

 

PROCESS ON VALUE-REQUEST.
  FIELD CUST_ID MODULE F4_CUST_ID.

This triggers the F4 popup when the user presses F4 on the ID field, showing a list of valid customer IDs to choose from.

Running the Program

  1. Execute STATUS_0100 with Direct Processing (F8).
  2. Enter the USER and PASSWORD credentials stored in ZMP_LOGIN.
  3. On successful login, Screen 0100 opens — showing the customer profile fields and the embedded subscreen with the Document Category input.
  4. Enter a Name, ID (use F4 for value help!), and Number, then click DISPLAY to fetch matching data from ZTMP_FY25.
  5. Enter a Document Category and click GET to navigate to Screen 0300 and view the EKKO table data in the table control.
  6. The custom DEMO menu in the menu bar lets you jump to SE41, SE51, or custom transactions directly.

 

 

 

Key Takeaways

ConceptWhat It DoesPBO ModuleRuns before screen renders; sets status, populates fieldsPAI ModuleRuns after user action; handles button clicks and navigationGUI StatusDefines menu bar, application toolbar, and function keysGUI TitleSets the screen title bar textSubscreenEmbeds one screen inside another screen areaTable ControlDisplays scrollable tabular data from an internal tablePOVRProcess On Value Request — enables F4 dropdown search helpCALL SCREENNavigates to another screen in the same programLEAVE TO SCREEN 0Exits the current screen stack back to the SAP menu

Wrapping Up

Okay, so — we built a login screen, a custom menu bar, a subscreen, a table view, and an F4 search help. From scratch. Inside SAP. Using nothing but ABAP, Screen Painter, and a lot of patience.

Honestly? It's one of the most fun things I've done in this language. There's something deeply satisfying about pressing F8 and seeing your own custom screen load up — with your menu, your buttons, your layout — inside the same SAP GUI that runs enterprise systems worldwide.

Will you hit errors along the way? Absolutely. The uppercase PF-STATUS rule will get you. The subscreen naming will confuse you for a bit. The Table Control loop logic will make you question your life choices for exactly 15 minutes. And then it'll work — and you'll feel great.

My advice: don't just read this. Open your SAP system and build it. Step by step, exactly as laid out. The muscle memory of actually doing it is worth ten times more than reading about it.

And when you're done — come back and tell me how it went. I'm serious. Drop a comment, share your output screenshot, ask questions. This stuff is more fun when we figure it out together. 🙌

entry.title: Step-by-Step Guide to Upport and Downport in SAP Using TCode UDO
entry.content:
Introduction:-

How I Use TCode UDO to Handle Downporting in SAP — A Practical Walkthrough:

I've spent a fair amount of time dealing with transport headaches in SAP landscapes where teams run parallel releases. One of the tools that's genuinely made my life easier in those situations is TCode UDO — the Upport/Downport Overview. I want to share how I actually use it, because the few times I went looking for a practical guide, I couldn't find one that covered the full flow from setup to verification.

Why I Needed UDO in the First Place:

The problem I kept running into: we'd develop a fix or a new function in a higher-release system, and then need to get it into a lower-release landscape for customers still on that version. The usual go-to tools — SCWB and SNOTE — handle code objects fine, but they fall short when your change involves DDICs, documentation, message classes, or table contents. I learned this the hard way after a failed downport left a data element missing in the target system and broke an F1 help link in production.

That's exactly the gap UDO fills. It generates a dedicated ABAP report (named UDO_NOTE_<SAPNoteNumber>, or RS_UDO_NOTE_<X> in BW) that gets packaged into a deliverable and shipped inside an SAP Note. When a customer applies that note via SNOTE, the report runs and handles all the DDIC and documentation creation automatically. Clean, auditable, and no manual recreation needed.
Here's a complete guide to SAP TCode UDO (Upport Downport Overview). Let's start with the landscape architecture — what the tool does at a system level.

Now here's the step-by-step interactive walkthrough of the exact actions inside TCode UDO.
The One Pre-Requisite That Catches People Out:

Before you even open UDO, you need an RFC connection between your source and target systems — and it has to be a Dialog User type connection, not a Background User. This is the thing that trips up new people most often.

If the connection type is wrong, UDO will fail silently or give confusing errors when it tries to compare object versions remotely. I always ask Basis to double-check this upfront. In SM59, the connection should look roughly like:

  • Connection type: 3 (ABAP connection)
  • Logon type: Dialog user ← this is the one that matters
  • Target host and system number pointing to the source system

Open TCode UDO in the target system

Log into the target system (the lower-release system you want to downport into). Open transaction UDO.

UDO — Upport Downport Overview (initial screen) 

Transport Request(s)           DEV K900123 ← TR from source
Source system                       DEV (client 100)
RFC destination                   SRCSYS_100
Only documentation            [ ] (unchecked = all objects)

You enter the TR number from the source system, but you run UDO from inside the target system. The RFC connection fetches the version comparison remotely.

Review the object list

UDO fetches all objects from the TR and displays them with their status in both systems. Each row shows whether the object exists locally and what copy mechanism will be used.

UDO — Object overview list 

Object                              Type                            Local                      Copy via
_______________________________________________________________

ZMAT_TAB                   TABD(table)                older                       UDO
ZMAT_PROG                PROG(report)             older                      SCWB
ZMAT_DE                      DTED(data el.)           missing                   UDO
ZMAT_DOC                   DOCU (F1 help)         missing                   UDO

Use the glasses icon to preview the object in the target, and the binoculars icon to preview in the source. The "Copy via" column shows whether UDO or SCWB handles the object — this is automatic.

Select objects and run simulation 

Select the rows you want to copy (or select all), then click the Copy Selected Objects button in the toolbar. UDO first runs a simulation (test run) before making any changes.

Simulation result — no actual changes yet 

Object                                Sim. status
________________________________________

ZMAT_TAB                       OK — will update
ZMAT_PROG                    OK — will copy
ZMAT_DE                          OK — will create 
ZMAT_DOC                       Warning — namespace

If the simulation shows errors, do not proceed. Fix the root cause (namespace, missing dependency, locked object) before continuing. 

Confirm and assign transport request 

If the simulation is clean, confirm the prompt. The system may ask you to assign a new transport request in the target system. This is the TR that will carry the downported objects.

Assign workbench request — target system 

Request                     QAS K200456 (auto-created)
Description               Downport: DEV K900123 feature 
Target                        QAS (quality assurance)

UDO performs two parallel actions: SCWB copies code-only objects; UDO itself handles DDIC, documentation, message classes, and table contents. Both results land in the same new TR.

UDO report is generated and shipped

UDO generates an ABAP program called UDO_NOTE_<SAPNoteNumber> (BW: RS_UDO_NOTE_<X>). This report is stored in the deliverable package and published inside the corresponding SAP Note on SAP ONE Support Portal.

Generated UDO report — SE38 in target 

Program name                UDO_NOTE_2367512
Package                           Normal deliverable package
TR                                    QAS K200456
Published in note            SAP Note #2367512

Customer systems download and execute this report via SNOTE (Note Assistant). When executed, it creates and activates all DDIC objects and documentation in the customer landscape automatically.

Monitor and verify:-

After the copy completes, verify all objects are correctly ported in the target system. 

SE11                   —     check DDIC objects exist and are active in the target system
SE80 / SE38       —     verify ABAP programs and function groups are present
SE63 / F1 help   —     confirm documentation is readable in target language
SE91                   —     check message classes and message texts are in place
SE10                   —     review the new TR and release it through the landscape as normal
STMS                 —     import the TR into QAS and then PRD via normal transport route

For upporting (lower to higher release), the same TCode and flow apply — just swap which system is source and which is target, and run UDO from the higher-release target system.

Key tools in the UDO ecosystem:-

TCode 

Role in the process 

SM59 

Create/verify the RFC dialog connection between systems 

UDO 

Main transaction — compare, simulate, and copy objects 

SE10 

Release the new TR created by UDO in the target system 

STMS 

Transport the released TR through QAS → PRD 

SNOTE 

Customers apply the generated UDO_NOTE_X report from SAP Notes 

SE11/SE80/SE91 

Verify DDIC, programs, message classes after porting 

How it works technically:

The UDO tool compares the active version of an ABAP object in the remote system (the source that has the feature) with the version in the local system (where the feature is to be ported). It then collects DDIC changes and documentation changes and prepares a new ABAP program, usually called UDO_NOTE_X or in BW area RS_UDO_NOTE_X, where X is the SAP Note number. 

The execution flow in the target system:  

In the target system, start transaction UDO. Enter the transport request from the source system in the Transport Request(s) field. If you only want to transfer documentation and not development objects, select the Only Documentation Objects checkbox. You can use the glasses icon to look at the object in the target and the binoculars icon to look at it in the source. Select the rows containing the objects you want to copy and click the Copy Selected Objects icon — UDO then performs a test (simulation) run first and shows what the results would be.

Conclusion:

In this blog post, we explored the complete process of performing Upport and Downport in SAP using Transaction Code UDO. We discussed how developers can efficiently transfer corrections and developments across different system landscapes while maintaining code consistency and minimizing manual effort.

Using TCode UDO simplifies transport synchronization between systems and helps teams manage parallel development activities more effectively. Understanding the Upport and Downport process is especially important in large SAP projects where multiple releases and support packages are handled simultaneously.

By following the steps and best practices covered in this guide, SAP developers and support teams can improve transport management, reduce inconsistencies, and ensure smoother deployment across environments.

entry.title: Re: Information about SAP MM and SAP Workflow
entry.content:
1) SAP MM (Materials Management)
MM is not a separate product; it’s a core module of SAP ERP (ECC) and S/4HANA. If the company already runs SAP ERP, MM functionality (procurement, inventory management, purchasing, contracts, etc.) is part of the standard system. What usually varies is the scope of implementation: some companies use only basic purchasing, while others activate advanced features like vendor evaluation, service procurement, or integration with production planning.

2) SAP Workflow
Workflow is a technical component embedded in SAP NetWeaver and S/4HANA. It is available in the system by default, but it needs to be configured and activated for specific business processes (e.g., purchase requisition approval, contract release). In S/4HANA Cloud, workflow is handled through Flexible Workflow (Fiori‑based, template‑driven). In on‑premise/private cloud, you can still use the classic workflow engine. No separate license is required for workflow itself, but consulting/configuration effort is needed to design and implement approval flows.

What this means for your Case Study

You don’t need to “buy” MM or Workflow separately; they are already part of the SAP ERP environment. The project effort is about activating, configuring, and redesigning processes to use these modules effectively. For procurement and contract approvals, the typical path is:

  • Use MM Purchasing for requisitions, RFQs, POs, and contracts.
  • Use Workflow/Flexible Workflow to route approvals to managers, finance, or legal before release.

entry.title: how to use "teams" within plant maintenance work centers when mulitple work centers but same "Team"!
entry.content:
@Gaayathri 

Greetings - we have a challenge within the setup of responsibility management, and the assignment of teams to Plant Maintenance Work Centers. I would be interested to recieve any suggestions of how we could setup the structures to enable the following.

We have two product lines, A and B. There is a team of 3 technicians for Product Line A, and a team of 2 for Product Line B. We want the list of "jobs" within the app Perform Jobs to list those available for the team based on Product Line.

Now, here is the rest of our setup

Product LineLocationCost CenterPlant Maintenance Work CenterTeam linking to "Perform Jobs" APP
ABuilding 00110001000-001Same team for product line A
ABuilding 00220002000-002Same team for product line A
BBuilding 00330003000-003Team for Product Line B

We have different work centers so that time booked is booked against the correct cost center based on location.

Here are the constriants we have come across when defining the teams, and the issues they create.

ConstraintsIssue
ONLY 1 team per Single Work Center - cannot allocate multiple work centers to a single teamNeed separate teams for work centers 1000-001 and 2000-002 - although the "team" of technicians is the same group of people - so they should be able to see all jobs for Product Line A, no matter what location.
ONLY able to assign member to 1 team, not against multiple teamsOnce a member is assigned say to the team linked with Work Center 1000-001 they cannot also be assigned to the team linked to Work Center 2000-002
Each team requires a memberIssue - once assign one of the tech team to one team, cannot assign to a second - so even if we used the team hierarchy we HAVE to assign someone to the lower level team, so they cannot then be assigned at the higher level in order to see all Product Line A maintenance jobs.

We want the technicians for Product Line A to view all the "team" jobs for product line A, but this does not seem possible given the team constraints mentioned above.

Does any one have any ideas/ suggestions of how we could proceed?

Many thanks

Adrian

entry.title: Re: Event-driven architecture
entry.content: We implemented a similar setup using SAP Event Mesh as the central event backbone and lightweight CAP services only where business logic or persistence was required. Topic-based subscriptions worked well for multiple downstream consumers and helped keep the architecture scalable and loosely coupled. Also worth considering idempotency handling early to avoid duplicate event processing in distributed scenarios.

entry.title: Re: Event-driven architecture
entry.content:
That’s actually a very good point. For lightweight transformations, routing, or integration-heavy scenarios, Integration Suite iFlows connected directly through Event Mesh queues can definitely be a cleaner and lower-maintenance approach.

I see CAP becoming more useful when the consumer needs custom business logic, persistence, aggregation, or API exposure. Otherwise, using iFlows as event consumers keeps the architecture simpler and avoids deploying additional application runtimes unnecessarily.

entry.title: Not able to add a new Routine Number inside the Pricing Procedure in the Drop down Group Condition
entry.content:
In the Above Screen shot we need a new Routine Number which can take some other Material Group into consider than what Sap standard has defined.

entry.title: Issue with Inbound Mail Processing in SAP
entry.content:
Dear Team,

We are facing an issue related to inbound mail processing in our SAP system.

We have already checked the following points:

  • SCOT configuration
  • SMICM service status
  • SICF service activation
  • User creation in SU01 with user type “System”
  • Network connectivity between SAP and mail server

Earlier, we were facing issues with outgoing mails as well; however, the outbound mail processing is now working properly. Despite all checks being completed successfully and no network issues being observed, inbound mails are still not being processed in SAP.

Request you to please help us identify the root cause and suggest the required solution.

Please let us know if any additional logs, traces, or configuration details are required from our end.

Regards,
Chetan Chavan
